text: In the operating room, the surgical team performs a side dock on the patient's right side. A 12mm camera port is placed in the left mid-abdomen, with two 8mm robotic ports on either side and a 5mm assistant port near Palmer's point, triangulating on the abscess cavity. The bladder is filled with methylene blue to help identify any urinary tract infection or bladder injury. The surgeon dissects the urachal cyst away from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully separating the peritoneum and fatty tissues surrounding the median umbilical ligament without violating the fascia.

The surgeon encounters calcification during dissection and sets it aside for removal later. Dissection proceeds along the peritoneum between the anterior abdominal wall and the median umbilical ligament, revealing a soft tissue plane between the urachal cyst and the bladder. The urachal cyst is carefully dissected free from the bladder, achieving hemostasis and avoiding bladder injury. The median umbilical ligament is isolated above the bladder and dissected free before being ligated using bipolar cautery and scissors.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the urachal cyst by removing the remaining tissue connecting it to the abdominal wall. The peritoneum is then closed with a single layer of interrupted vicryl sutures.
